A.missing: after death heir only CAN think 'mine' — the entitlement to possession is dropped, and heir=1 is never tied to the dead person
A.reason: vague THINK-mine rather than actual passing of possession; missing the link between deceased and heir.

B.missing: law/will basis of entitlement is soft, but the criterial transfer is present
B.reason: another dies, the thing was theirs, after death the thing is with heir who can say 'mine' — captures inheritance.

C.missing: 'someone says the thing is at heir' is a wobbly proxy for entitlement; possession-transfer is present but muddled
C.reason: thing was with the dead one before, moves to heir after death; captures core transfer despite convoluted SAY clauses.

D.missing: no actual death event or transfer of possession; only heir's belief that 'when he dies the thing will be mine'
D.reason: reduces to heir's thought about future possession; no realized inheritance.

E.missing: 'after death heir CAN DO the thing' loses possession/ownership entirely — generic ability, not entitlement to inherit estate
E.reason: too generic; CAN DO with a thing after someone dies fits many concepts.

F.missing: thing moves to heir after death, but no possession/ownership ('mine') and no prior ownership by deceased
F.reason: bare movement of a thing to a person after a death; underspecifies inheritance as ownership transfer.

G.missing: law/will entitlement soft; deceased's prior ownership only implied via 'other thinks mine'
G.reason: another owned things, dies, after that the things are the heir's — renders the transfer of estate.
